meta.title: Expanded material and ingredient notes
meta.note: These entries extend the root materials pack with more specific workshop-facing descriptions for materials that already appear in the codebase or in recipe references.

materials.name: EPK kaolin
whatItAdds: reliable alumina and silica with good bucket suspension
a familiar default kaolin for cone-6 and cone-10 studio work
bestUses: general clear and liner glazes
recipes where handling and suspension matter as much as whiteness
watchFor: slightly warmer fired cast than Grolleg in white or transparent systems
too much raw kaolin can increase drying shrinkage and crawling risk
switchingNotes: move toward Grolleg when colour purity matters more than suspension strength
split with calcined kaolin when the dry glaze layer is cracking before firing
materials.typicalUsePercent: 10-25% in many clear, satin, and matte bases

materials.name: Grolleg kaolin
whatItAdds: very white clay contribution with lower titanium contamination than ordinary kaolins
cleaner fired colour in porcelain-oriented hosts
bestUses: porcelain clears
chrome-tin and other colour-sensitive hosts
watchFor: less forgiving bucket behaviour than a more plastic kaolin
higher cost for benefits that only matter in some glaze families
switchingNotes: switch from EPK when a clear is good but still too warm on porcelain
test suspension after the swap because the cleaner material may brush and settle differently

materials.name: Custer feldspar
whatItAdds: the familiar North American high-fire feldspar backbone
a balanced K2O and Na2O flux package with alumina and silica
bestUses: cone-10 gloss and matte work
traditional feldspathic bodies and glazes
watchFor: brand-era changes matter when comparing old recipes to current batches
blind substitution with G-200 HP or nepheline syenite changes fit direction
switchingNotes: test side by side with G-200 HP rather than assuming the old recipe language still means the same thing
if replacing with nepheline syenite, expect a more aggressive melt and higher expansion direction

materials.name: G-200 HP feldspar
whatItAdds: a potash-rich feldspar alternative to Custer
slightly different K2O to Na2O balance than older G-200 or current Custer
bestUses: updating legacy North American recipes
direct comparison tests against Custer when reproducing older cone-10 surfaces
watchFor: historical recipes written for original G-200 may not map cleanly to current HP material
small chemistry shifts can still move colour break and fit
switchingNotes: run a two-batch comparison rather than a one-batch substitution if the surface matters
treat the swap as a flux-balance test, not only a supply substitution

materials.name: Cornwall stone
whatItAdds: a traditional British combined flux and silica-bearing stone
a stiffer, more silica-rich feel than many modern feldspar-only routes
bestUses: traditional high-fire or ash-adjacent glaze lineages
comparing natural feldspathic character against cleaner industrial flux packages
watchFor: regional availability and analysis variation matter
it is not a straight feldspar replacement because it brings extra silica and calcium
switchingNotes: when replacing with feldspar, check whether silica also needs to rise
when replacing feldspar with Cornwall stone, expect a stiffer melt unless other fluxes compensate

materials.name: Colemanite
whatItAdds: boron with more calcium and less sodium than many frit routes
a more mineral-style melt path than a fully fritted boron source
bestUses: legacy low and mid-fire recipes where a natural borate direction is part of the surface
comparison tests against Gerstley Borate rebuilds
watchFor: still a natural mineral with batch personality
high LOI means firing and bubble release still matter
switchingNotes: often closer to old borate behaviour than a single frit, but still not a universal Gerstley replacement
test against a frit rebuild rather than trusting a simple 1:1 swap

materials.name: Soda ash
whatItAdds: a strong sodium push with no extra silica or alumina
classic carbon-trap and shino-style surface behaviour when the system supports it
bestUses: special-effect high-fire work
small controlled additions where sodium response itself is the goal
watchFor: it is soluble and migrates during drying
it can cause hard rims, uneven surfaces, and application instability
switchingNotes: if the drying behaviour becomes unmanageable, rebuild part of the sodium direction with feldspar or frit rather than forcing more soda ash
always separate bucket-behaviour problems from fired-chemistry goals

materials.name: Epsom salt
whatItAdds: flocculation control rather than meaningful glaze chemistry
a quick way to tighten a watery or hard-settling glaze slurry
bestUses: bucket rescue for deflocculated or poorly suspended glazes
small process adjustments without rewriting the recipe
watchFor: overuse can gel the bucket and create false confidence that the recipe itself is healthy
it solves application behaviour, not fit or melt problems
switchingNotes: add it as a process correction only after checking water amount and sieve discipline
document the amount because flocculation changes brushing and thickness quickly
materials.typicalUsePercent: trace additions dissolved in water rather than recipe-level percentages

materials.name: Black iron oxide
whatItAdds: a darker iron route that can melt slightly differently than red iron
dense brown to black direction in temmoku or dark gloss systems
bestUses: dark high-iron glazes
substituting down from red iron when a recipe is too rusty or too warm
watchFor: it is not just a cosmetic colour swap for red iron
it can make the same glaze slightly more fluid or darker at a lower loading
switchingNotes: start lower than the red-iron amount when converting an established recipe
test the same glaze on vertical tiles because the melt can shift slightly

materials.name: Manganese carbonate
whatItAdds: a somewhat milder manganese route than manganese dioxide
brown, plum, and muted purple shifts especially in combination systems
bestUses: purple-brown development with cobalt
special-effect dark glazes where the studio wants a softer manganese move
watchFor: health and ventilation concerns remain real even if the material feels softer to use
manganese can affect fit and surface, not only colour
switchingNotes: when swapping from manganese dioxide, start with a small comparison rather than equal weight
document kiln ventilation and firing context because safety is part of the test
materials.typicalUsePercent: 1-6% in most studio glaze colour work

materials.name: Copper oxide
whatItAdds: the same essential copper chemistry as carbonate with more strength per gram
a slightly leaner route when the recipe already carries too much carbonate gas
bestUses: switching a copper glaze away from excess LOI
strong oxidation turquoise or reduction metallic testing
watchFor: it is stronger than copper carbonate and can overdrive the glaze quickly
copper safety and food-surface caution do not disappear when the material form changes
switchingNotes: reduce the loading relative to copper carbonate and test side by side
separate colour gain from melt change because the lower LOI can also alter the surface
materials.typicalUsePercent: 0.75-4.5% in most oxidation work, higher only in special-effect systems

materials.name: Nickel oxide
whatItAdds: muted grey, taupe, blue-grey, and olive shifts depending on the host
a strong modifier role even when the colour is subtle
bestUses: quieting a bright colour family
developing smoky blue-grey or complex neutrals
watchFor: results vary more by host glaze than many potters expect
it is better treated as a modifier than as a guaranteed solo colour
switchingNotes: test it in a stable transparent host first so you can see what the nickel is doing
avoid rewriting the whole base until you know whether the colour issue is the nickel or the host

materials.name: Ilmenite
whatItAdds: iron-titanium speckle and broken dark spotting
a more particulate surface event than fine iron or rutile additions
bestUses: speckled matte and gloss surfaces
adding visible particulate texture without changing the whole recipe family
watchFor: particle size matters as much as chemistry
heavy additions can roughen the surface or overwhelm the glaze
switchingNotes: test the coarse grade separately from fine powder because they behave almost like different materials visually
keep the host stable so the speckle effect can be judged honestly
materials.typicalUsePercent: 2-8% depending on particle size and desired speckle density

materials.name: Strontium carbonate
whatItAdds: a lower-toxicity route when a recipe historically used barium for surface reasons
a stable alkaline-earth flux that can support gloss or crystal response
bestUses: rebuilding older barium recipes more conservatively
surface experiments where a different alkaline-earth response is desired
watchFor: it is safer than barium in workflow terms but not chemically identical
blind swapping can flatten or redirect the original surface
switchingNotes: treat it as a new recipe branch when replacing barium rather than a box-check substitution
watch gloss, colour, and fit together because all three can shift
